mission.title: Install and Configure Longhorn on Kubernetes
mission.description: Longhorn is a cloud-native distributed block storage system for Kubernetes, providing reliable and powerful storage solutions. Installing Longhorn allows you to enhance your Kubernetes cluster with persistent volume support and enterprise-grade storage features.

steps.title: Add the Longhorn Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Longhorn Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add longhorn https://charts.longhorn.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Longhorn using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Longhorn in the 'longhorn-system' namespace with the following command:
```bash
helm install longhorn longhorn/longhorn --namespace longhorn-system --create-namespace --version v1.11.0
```

steps.title: Verify Longhorn installation
steps.description:
Check that all Longhorn pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system
```

steps.title: Access Longhorn UI
steps.description:
To access the Longhorn UI, you can port-forward the service to your local machine:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/longhorn-frontend -n longhorn-system 8080:80
```
You can now access the Longhorn UI by navigating to http://localhost:8080 in your web browser.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Longhorn will show all pods in the 'longhorn-system' namespace as running. You can access the Longhorn UI at http://localhost:8080 and configure your storage settings as needed.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add longhorn https://charts.longhorn.io
helm repo update
helm install longhorn longhorn/longhorn --namespace longhorn-system --create-namespace --version v1.11.0

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Longhorn installation, first uninstall the Helm release using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall longhorn --namespace longhorn-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and any persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created by Longhorn:
```bash
kubectl delete crd volumes.longhorn.io
kubectl delete crd engines.longhorn.io
kubectl delete crd replicas.longhorn.io
kubectl delete crd settings.longhorn.io
kubectl delete pvc --all -n longhorn-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the Longhorn namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace longhorn-system
kubectl get namespaces | grep longhorn-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading Longhorn, it's important to back up your current state. You can create a backup of your existing Longhorn volumes using:
```bash
kubectl get volumes.longhorn.io -n longhorn-system -o yaml > longhorn-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Longhorn Helm repository and upgrade the installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade longhorn longhorn/longhorn --namespace longhorn-system --version v1.12.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that all Longhorn pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see Longhorn pods in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the failing pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n longhorn-system
```
Look for error messages that indicate the cause of the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Longhorn UI not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Longhorn UI, ensure that the port-forward command is running. If it is, check if the service is up:
```bash
kubectl get svc longhorn-frontend -n longhorn-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Volume not attaching
troubleshooting.description:
If a volume is not attaching to a pod, check the volume status:
```bash
kubectl get volumes.longhorn.io -n longhorn-system
```
Ensure that the volume is in 'attached' state and check for any events related to the volume.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources error
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an 'insufficient resources' error, verify the resource limits set for Longhorn components and adjust them if necessary:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment longhorn-manager -n longhorn-system
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
